template:
**DOCUMENT FORMAT — MASSACHUSETTS PERSONAL-INJURY MOTION TO DISMISS**:
A Rule 12(b) motion filed in a Massachusetts civil action. Court-ready, substantive, with case law and pin cites. Typically 6–12 pages including Memorandum of Law.

**DYNAMIC BEHAVIOR — READ documentOptions**:
- `grounds`: one or more of `12b1` (subject-matter jurisdiction), `12b2` (personal jurisdiction), `12b5` (insufficient service), `12b6` (failure to state a claim), `statute_of_limitations`, `workers_compensation_exclusivity`, `mtca_notice` (Mass. Tort Claims Act presentment failure — public-entity defendants), `charitable_immunity_cap` (M.G.L. c. 231, § 85K damages cap for charitable institutions), `coemployee_bar` (Panagakos bar to co-employee negligence suits).
- `court`: `superior` (default) | `district` | `bmc`.
- `includeMemorandum`: boolean (default true) — when true, follow the motion with a `MEMORANDUM OF LAW IN SUPPORT`.
- For any `grounds` value NOT selected, suppress the corresponding ARGUMENT sub-section entirely — do not emit a placeholder heading.

**CAPTION** (exactly this format — no text above):

COMMONWEALTH OF MASSACHUSETTS
[COUNTY], SS.                                                               [COURT NAME]
                                                                            CIVIL ACTION NO.: _________

[PLAINTIFF],                                                  )
                       Plaintiff,                             )
                                                              )    DEFENDANT'S MOTION TO
v.                                                            )    DISMISS PURSUANT TO
                                                              )    MASS. R. CIV. P. 12(b)
[DEFENDANT],                                                  )
                       Defendant.                             )

**STRUCTURE**:

## DEFENDANT'S MOTION TO DISMISS

Pursuant to Mass. R. Civ. P. 12(b)[applicable subsection(s)], Defendant [Name] respectfully moves this Honorable Court for an Order dismissing the Complaint in its entirety, for the reasons stated in the accompanying Memorandum of Law.

WHEREFORE, Defendant respectfully requests that this Court:
1. ALLOW Defendant's Motion to Dismiss;
2. Dismiss the Complaint with prejudice; and
3. Grant such other and further relief as this Court deems just.

Respectfully submitted,
[Attorney Name], Esq.
BBO #[number]
[Firm]
[Address]
[Phone] | [Email]

ATTORNEY FOR DEFENDANT

---

## MEMORANDUM OF LAW IN SUPPORT OF DEFENDANT'S MOTION TO DISMISS

### I. INTRODUCTION
One paragraph previewing each ground for dismissal and the relief sought.

### II. FACTUAL AND PROCEDURAL BACKGROUND
Briefly (1–2 paragraphs) summarize only those facts alleged in the Complaint that are relevant to the motion. Under Iannacchino v. Ford Motor Co., 451 Mass. 623, 636 (2008), the Court accepts the well-pleaded facts as true for 12(b)(6) purposes but disregards legal conclusions. Cite this standard for 12(b)(6).

### III. ARGUMENT

Use lettered sub-sections (**A.**, **B.**, **C.**) per ground. For each ground:

**A. 12(b)(6) — FAILURE TO STATE A CLAIM**
The pleading standard requires 'factual allegations plausibly suggesting (not merely consistent with) an entitlement to relief.' Iannacchino, 451 Mass. at 636 (adopting the Twombly/Iqbal plausibility standard); see also Curtis v. Herb Chambers I-95, Inc., 458 Mass. 674, 676 (2011). Apply to the specific counts in the Complaint and explain why each fails.

**B. STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS — M.G.L. c. 260, § 2A**
Personal-injury tort claims must be brought within three years of the cause of action's accrual. M.G.L. c. 260, § 2A. Where the limitations defect is apparent on the face of the Complaint, dismissal under Rule 12(b)(6) is proper. See Epstein v. C.R. Bard, Inc., 460 F.3d 183, 187 (1st Cir. 2006) (applying Massachusetts law). Apply to the specific dates alleged.

**C. WORKERS' COMPENSATION EXCLUSIVITY — M.G.L. c. 152, §§ 23, 24**
When the injury arises in the course of employment and the employer is covered by workers' compensation insurance, the Workers' Compensation Act is the exclusive remedy. See Foley v. Polaroid Corp., 381 Mass. 545, 548 (1980); Saharceski v. Marcure, 373 Mass. 304, 311 (1977).

**D. 12(b)(1) — LACK OF SUBJECT-MATTER JURISDICTION** [include only if selected]
The Court's subject-matter jurisdiction is limited by statute. See M.G.L. c. 212, § 3 (Superior Court amount-in-controversy floor); M.G.L. c. 218, § 19A (District Court ceiling). Apply to the specific jurisdictional defect — e.g., amount in controversy, exclusive tribunal jurisdiction (med mal tribunal under M.G.L. c. 231, § 60B), or preemption. Subject-matter jurisdiction is never waived and may be raised at any time. Blixt v. Blixt, 437 Mass. 649, 652 n.2 (2002).

**E. 12(b)(2) — LACK OF PERSONAL JURISDICTION** [include only if selected]
Personal jurisdiction requires both statutory authority under the Massachusetts long-arm statute, M.G.L. c. 223A, § 3, and compliance with federal due process. See Roberts v. Legendary Marine Sales, 447 Mass. 860, 863 (2006). Identify which long-arm subsection is invoked (or argue none applies) and address minimum contacts under International Shoe Co. v. Washington, 326 U.S. 310, 316 (1945), and Burger King Corp. v. Rudzewicz, 471 U.S. 462, 474-76 (1985).

**F. 12(b)(5) — INSUFFICIENT SERVICE OF PROCESS** [include only if selected]
Service must conform to Mass. R. Civ. P. 4. Apply to the specific defect — e.g., service on wrong party, service after expiration of the Rule 4(j) 90-day period, failure to serve the registered agent of a corporate defendant under Mass. R. Civ. P. 4(d)(2), or out-of-state service not authorized by M.G.L. c. 223A, § 6.

**G. MASSACHUSETTS TORT CLAIMS ACT — PRESENTMENT FAILURE** [include only if `mtca_notice` selected]
Suits against public employers must satisfy the presentment requirement of M.G.L. c. 258, § 4: written presentment to the executive officer of the public employer within two (2) years of the date of injury, and a suit filed no later than three (3) years from that date. Presentment is a statutory condition precedent, not a statute of limitations. Weaver v. Commonwealth, 387 Mass. 43, 47 (1982); Vasys v. Metro. Dist. Comm'n, 387 Mass. 51, 55 (1982). Apply to the facts — identify the absence, untimeliness, or inadequacy of the presentment letter.

**H. CHARITABLE IMMUNITY — DAMAGES CAP** [include only if `charitable_immunity_cap` selected]
Tort recoveries against charitable corporations are capped at $20,000 when the cause of action arises from activities carried on to accomplish the institution's charitable purposes. M.G.L. c. 231, § 85K. The cap does not eliminate the claim but limits recoverable damages; where the Complaint pleads damages in excess of the cap and the defendant is a qualifying charity, the Court may narrow the case at the pleading stage. See English v. New England Med. Ctr., Inc., 405 Mass. 423, 425 (1989) (scope of § 85K).

**I. WORKERS' COMPENSATION CO-EMPLOYEE BAR** [include only if `coemployee_bar` selected]
The Workers' Compensation Act bars negligence suits between co-employees injured in the course of common employment. Saharceski v. Marcure, 373 Mass. 304, 311 (1977); Panagakos v. Walsh, 434 Mass. 353, 355-56 (2001). The bar extends to ordinary negligence; only intentional torts fall outside it. Apply to the alleged employer-employee relationship and the scope-of-employment facts.

### IV. CONCLUSION
For the reasons stated above, this Court should ALLOW Defendant's Motion to Dismiss with prejudice.

Respectfully submitted,
[Signature block — suppress if stationery is applied]

### CERTIFICATE OF SERVICE
I hereby certify that a true and correct copy of the foregoing DEFENDANT'S MOTION TO DISMISS (and accompanying Memorandum of Law) was served on counsel for Plaintiff on [date] by electronic filing and service via the eFileMA system pursuant to Superior Court Standing Order 4-18. [If eFileMA is unavailable to a party — e.g., self-represented litigant — fall back to: 'first-class mail postage prepaid to counsel for Plaintiff at [address].']

[Attorney Name]

**CONSTRAINTS**:
- Cite Mass. R. Civ. P. exactly, NOT 'Massachusetts Rules of Civil Procedure.'
- Cite MA statutes exactly as `M.G.L. c. X, § Y` (e.g., `M.G.L. c. 260, § 2A`). Do not use variants like 'G.L.' or 'Mass. Gen. Laws.'
- Use BBO # (Massachusetts convention).
- Each ground for dismissal gets its OWN lettered sub-section. Do not blend 12(b)(6) and SOL into one section.
- Pin cites are REQUIRED for every case citation — the specific page the proposition comes from, immediately after the volume and reporter (e.g., `447 Mass. 141, 146 (2006)`).
- For any `documentOptions.grounds` value NOT selected, suppress the corresponding ARGUMENT sub-section entirely. Never emit an empty or placeholder-only section.
- If firm stationery is applied, SUPPRESS the signature blocks after WHEREFORE and after the Memorandum CONCLUSION — the stationery handles them.
- Target length: 6–12 pages including the Memorandum of Law. The motion itself is 1–2 pages; the Memorandum carries the balance.
- Do NOT include a Table of Authorities unless documentOptions.includeTableOfAuthorities = true.
hints: Massachusetts PI 12(b) motion to dismiss. COMMONWEALTH OF MASSACHUSETTS caption, BBO # convention. Memorandum of Law follows the motion. Default cert of service is eFileMA under Superior Court Standing Order 4-18. PI-specific grounds: MTCA presentment failure (M.G.L. c. 258, § 4), charitable immunity $20K cap (M.G.L. c. 231, § 85K), co-employee bar (Saharceski/Panagakos). Key cases: Iannacchino v. Ford (12(b)(6) plausibility), M.G.L. c. 260 § 2A (3-year PI SOL), M.G.L. c. 152 §§ 23, 24 (workers' comp exclusivity). Pin cites required. Lettered sub-sections per ground (A/B/C/...); suppress unselected grounds entirely.

Roberts v. Legendary Marine Sales, 447 Mass. 860, 863 (2006)
English v. New England Med. Ctr., Inc., 405 Mass. 423, 425 (1989)

disclaimer: DRAFT — generated from a template. Requires review by a licensed attorney in the applicable jurisdiction before filing, sending, or relying upon. No attorney-client relationship is created by the generation of this document. Not legal advice.
knownLimitations: Does not account for local court rules (county- or judge-specific).
Statute citations verified as of lastVerified date only; confirm against current statute before use.
Strategic positioning (what to demand, what to concede) is outside template scope — attorney judgment required.
Motion-deadline calculations (Rule 12 response periods, court-set schedules) require attorney verification against the operative scheduling order.
Does not include Table of Authorities generation — if required by local rule, attorney must compile separately.
